rounds.blurb: New Orleans under water in the days after Hurricane Katrina.
rounds.credit: Cmdr. Mark Moran / NOAA — public domain
rounds.story: The levees failed in more than 50 places and 80% of New Orleans went underwater for weeks. Katrina killed more than 1,800 people and remains the costliest hurricane in U.S. history.

rounds.blurb: Pluto in true color, captured by New Horizons during humanity's first flyby of the dwarf planet.
rounds.story: New Horizons flew nine and a half years and three billion miles for a flyby lasting hours, revealing a heart-shaped nitrogen glacier on a world demoted from planethood mid-journey. Discoverer Clyde Tombaugh's ashes rode aboard.

rounds.blurb: The Space Needle rises over the Century 21 Exposition — the Seattle World's Fair.
rounds.story: Built in about 400 days for the fair, the Space Needle anchored an exposition themed on life in the year 2000 — ten million visitors rode the new monorail and previewed touch-tone phones. Elvis filmed a movie on the grounds that fall.

rounds.blurb: Falcon Heavy's first flight leaves Pad 39A — with a Roadster for a payload.
rounds.story: The demo flight carried a Tesla Roadster with a spacesuited mannequin at the wheel, now orbiting the Sun beyond Mars. Both side boosters landed back at the Cape in formation — the moment reusable rockets stopped looking experimental.

rounds.blurb: Voyager 2 leaves Earth aboard a Titan IIIE — carrying the Golden Record toward interstellar space.
rounds.story: Voyager 2 actually launched sixteen days before Voyager 1, on a slower path that let it visit all four outer planets — an alignment that comes once every 176 years. Nearly five decades on, it still calls home from interstellar space.
